turns.content: alright so I need a new combi drill and I'm stuck between Makita and DeWalt innit. been going back and forth for about a week now and I can't decide. what do you reckon

Both are solid brands so you can't really go wrong, but it depends on what you prioritize. A few questions to help narrow it down:

1. Are you already invested in either battery platform? If you've got Makita or DeWalt batteries from other tools, sticking with that system saves you money.
2. What are you mainly using it for — drilling through masonry, wood, general fixing? Heavy daily use or occasional?
3. Budget — are you looking at the mid-range 18V models or the top-end brushless ones?

good questions. I've got a mix of both actually which is proper annoying. got a Makita impact driver and a DeWalt circular saw so neither battery system is dominant innit. mainly using the drill for drilling through walls for pipe brackets and radiator fixings so it needs decent hammer action. and I use it every single day so it needs to be tough. budget wise I'll go brushless, no point buying cheap when I'm using it daily

Right, so daily heavy use with hammer action for masonry — that narrows it down nicely. Here's how they compare in the brushless 18V range:

**Makita DHP486** (or the newer DHP487)
- Really well balanced, lighter in the hand — matters a lot when you're using it overhead all day
- Smooth trigger control, very precise at low speeds
- Makita tends to be slightly more compact, good for tight spaces behind boilers and under sinks
- Battery life is excellent on the 5.0Ah packs

**DeWalt DCD796** (or the DCD805)
- Slightly more torque in most tests — hits harder in hammer mode
- Feels chunkier and more robust, some people prefer that solid feel
- DeWalt's XR batteries are tanks, arguably the best longevity in the industry
- Three-speed gearbox on some models gives you more versatility

For plumbing work specifically — tight spaces, overhead work, precision drilling for brackets — the Makita's lighter weight and compact size is usually the winner. But if raw hammer power through masonry is the priority, DeWalt edges it.

see that's exactly my problem innit. I want the Makita cos its lighter and I'm in tight spots all day but I also want the DeWalt hammer action cos some of these old Victorian walls are like drilling through concrete. actually forget the torque specs I can look that up. what I really want to know is which one holds up better long term. like after 2 years of daily abuse which one is still going strong

That's the more important question honestly. From what tradespeople generally report:

**Makita** tends to have better motor longevity. The brushless motors in the DHP range are known for lasting well beyond 3-4 years of daily use. The gearbox is smooth and doesn't develop play as quickly. Where Makita sometimes lets you down is the chuck — some models develop wobble after heavy hammer use, which is annoying but replaceable.

**DeWalt** builds an absolutely bulletproof body. The housing takes drops and knocks better than almost anything. The chuck holds up well under hammer action. The weak point tends to be the trigger switch — after a couple of years of constant use, some people report the variable speed trigger getting inconsistent.

Both are repairable and parts are easy to get, which matters more than anything when you rely on a tool daily.

Honestly, for your use case — tight spaces, daily hammer drilling into old brick — I'd lean Makita for the weight and compactness, and just plan to replace the chuck if it goes after a couple of years. A chuck replacement is cheap. Fatigue from a heavier drill every day is not.

that makes sense actually. the weight thing is real cos by Friday my wrist is killing me from holding the drill up all week innit. and you're right I can swap a chuck out in 10 minutes its nothing. sorted on that front then. what about price, where am I getting the best deal right now

For the best deals on the Makita brushless combis right now, your main options are:

- **Screwfix** — they often do bundle deals with two batteries and a case. Worth checking their current offers, especially if there's a seasonal sale on.
- **Toolstation** — competitive pricing and sometimes cheaper than Screwfix on the same model.
- **Amazon** — can be hit or miss, but sometimes you'll find a body-only deal if you've already got Makita batteries. Just make sure it's sold by an authorized dealer so the warranty is valid.
- **eBay (new from trade sellers)** — sometimes 10-15% cheaper than retail. Again, check the seller is authorized.

If you can wait a few weeks, there's usually a spring sale push from the big trade suppliers around April/May. But if you need it now, Screwfix bundle deals are usually the best value because you get the batteries included.

Also worth checking if your local plumbing merchant does tool deals — some of them have trade accounts with Makita and can get you a better price than retail.

I've got a Screwfix trade card so I might get something extra off. right I think I'm going Makita. the weight thing sealed it for me. plus my impact driver is Makita so at least some of my batteries will be compatible. cheers for talking me through it mate I've been going round in circles on this for days

Good shout. And the battery compatibility with your impact driver is actually a bigger deal than people think — being able to grab a charged battery from one tool and stick it in another without thinking about it saves time on the job. You're basically consolidating toward Makita as your main platform now, which makes every future tool purchase simpler too.

Enjoy the new drill. Your wrists will thank you by next Friday.

yeah that's a brilliant point actually. having one battery system just makes life easier innit. no more fumbling around in the van trying to figure out which charger goes with which tool. half the time I'm grabbing the wrong one anyway

Exactly — the less you have to think about on a job, the better. One battery platform means one charger type, batteries are interchangeable, and you can carry fewer spares. It sounds small but over a full working week it adds up to less hassle and less weight in the van.

Once you've got the drill and the impact driver both on Makita, you'll probably find yourself naturally picking Makita for the next tool too. That's how the ecosystem locks you in — but in a good way when you're already happy with the quality.

yeah that's how they get you isn't it haha. once you're in the ecosystem you're committed. bit like phones innit, once you're on iPhone you're not switching. anyway do you reckon I should get the bare unit or the full kit with the batteries and case

Since you've already got Makita batteries from your impact driver, the bare unit might be the smarter buy — you'll save a decent chunk of money and you've already got batteries and a charger that work. No point paying for kit you don't need.

The only reason to go full kit is if your current batteries are getting old and losing charge, or if you want a couple of extra batteries in rotation so you've always got one charged. If your existing ones are still holding up fine, bare unit all the way.

my batteries are still going strong so bare unit it is then. sorted. right I'm ordering it tonight before I change my mind again haha. cheers mate, brilliant help as always. no more going back and forth, Makita it is

Ha, order it quick before the analysis paralysis kicks back in. You've made a solid choice — lighter drill, battery compatibility, good long-term durability. Ticks all the boxes for what you need.

Let me know how it feels on the first day out. Enjoy the new tool.
